subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Look up. Every single day of your life, the Sun climbs into the sky over the Indian Ocean, warms the savanna, and later slips down behind the western hills. Every month the Moon grows from a thin silver curve into a bright full circle, then shrinks away again. When your family watches for the new crescent moon to know that Ramadan or Eid has begun, you are doing exactly what scientists and travellers have done for thousands of years - reading the sky. In this unit you are going to discover the real story behind these everyday wonders.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Science 0097 — Stage 5 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit you will be able to:
Explain that Earth is a planet that travels around the Sun, together with seven other planets in our Solar System.
Describe how Earth spinning on its axis causes the cycle of day and night.
Explain why the Sun appears to move across the sky from east to west, even though the Sun is not really moving around us.
Describe how Earth's journey around the Sun takes one year (about 365 days).
Explain why the Moon appears to change shape through its phases, and connect this to the lunar (Hijri) calendar.
Name the eight planets in order and describe how the Solar System is arranged.
Use the Sun and stars to work out direction, and understand how people have navigated with the sky.
Carry out safe home investigations using a ball and a torch to model day, night and the Moon's phases.
Here are the important words for this unit. Say each one aloud, then use it when you talk about the sky with your family or your AI tutor.

concepts.title: Our Home in Space
concepts.explanation: Let us begin with a big question: what is your address in the universe? You might start with your house, then your town - perhaps Mogadishu, Kismaayo or Dar es Salaam - then your country, then the continent of Africa. But you can keep going. Africa sits on the planet Earth. Earth is one of eight planets that travel around a star we call the Sun. The Sun and everything travelling around it make up our Solar System. And our Solar System is just one tiny corner of a giant collection of billions of stars called a…
concepts.example: There are eight planets. In order from the Sun they are: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus and Neptune. A helpful way to remember the order is to make up a sentence where each word begins with the…

concepts.title: Day and Night: Earth's Spin
concepts.explanation: Now think about a single day. In the morning the Sun rises in the east, over the Indian Ocean if you live on the coast. By midday it is high overhead - this is noon. In the evening it sinks in the west and sets, and then comes night. It really looks as if the Sun is travelling across the sky above us. But it is not. Here is the true explanation. Earth spins like a top. This spinning is called rotation. Earth turns all the way around once every 24 hours, which is exactly why a day is 24 hours long. Earth spins…
concepts.example: The Sun shines on Earth from one side only, just as a torch shines on one side of a ball. So at any moment, half of Earth is lit (that half has daytime) and half is dark (that half has night-time). As Earth rotates,…

concepts.title: The Year: Earth's Journey Around the Sun
concepts.explanation: While Earth is spinning to give us day and night, it is also slowly travelling all the way around the Sun. One full trip around the Sun takes about 365 days - and that is exactly what we call a year. So Earth is doing two movements at the same time: it is rotating (spinning once a day) and orbiting (circling the Sun once a year). Think of a spinning top that someone is also carrying slowly around a table. A year is actually a little longer than 365 days - it is closer to 365 and a quarter days. Those extra…
concepts.example: Earth's axis is tilted - it leans over at an angle rather than standing straight up. As Earth journeys around the Sun, this tilt means that different parts of the planet lean towards the Sun at different times of the…

concepts.title: The Moon and Its Phases
concepts.explanation: Now let us turn to Earth's closest companion in space: the Moon. The Moon is a natural satellite, which means it travels around Earth, just as Earth travels around the Sun. The Moon is much smaller than Earth - about one quarter of Earth's width - and it is our nearest neighbour in space, though still very far away. It takes the Moon about 29.5 days to travel once around Earth. Here is something surprising: the Moon does not make its own light. It is not glowing like the Sun. The reason the Moon looks bright is…
concepts.example: Let us walk through the main phases in order. At new moon, the Moon sits between Earth and the Sun, so its dark side faces us and we can barely see it at all. A day or two later, a thin bright curve appears - the waxing…

concepts.title: Stars, Constellations and Finding Your Way
concepts.explanation: On a clear night far from city lights - out in the open savanna - the sky fills with countless tiny points of light. These are stars, and every one of them is a Sun of its own: a huge ball of hot, glowing gas making its own light. They look tiny only because they are unimaginably far away. Light from even the nearest star (after our Sun) takes more than four years to reach your eyes. Our Sun looks big and bright only because it is so much closer to us than all the others. Long ago, people noticed that certain…
concepts.example: Stars, like the Sun, seem to move slowly across the sky through the night, rising in the east and setting in the west. Once again, this is because Earth is rotating, not because the stars are moving around us. And…

concepts.title: Exploring Space
concepts.explanation: How do we know so much about places we can never visit? People have built clever tools to explore space. Telescopes gather and focus light so we can see distant objects far more clearly than with our eyes alone. Some telescopes sit on the ground; others have been launched above the air, where the view is even sharper. Satellites are machines that we send to orbit Earth. Weather satellites help forecasters warn coastal towns like Mombasa and Kismaayo about storms; communication satellites carry phone calls and the…
concepts.example: Sending messages across space is slow because of the enormous distances. A radio signal from a rover on Mars can take several minutes to reach Earth, so we cannot steer it moment by moment as we would a toy car - the…

explorations.title: Making Day and Night With a Ball and a Torch
explorations.context: To model how Earth's spinning causes day and night, and why the Sun seems to move across the sky.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write down your idea: As the ball turns, will the whole ball be lit at once, or only one side at a time? What do you think will happen to a marked spot as it turns?
explorations.answer: How much of the ball was lit at any one moment? What does this tell you about Earth? Did the torch (the Sun) move at all? What was actually moving? As the ball turned, which way did the light seem to 'travel' across your town - the same way the ball turned or the opposite way? Use your model to…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To model how Earth's spinning causes day and night, and why the Sun seems to move across the sky.

explorations.title: Modelling the Phases of the Moon
explorations.context: To model how the Moon appears to change shape, and to see why the Moon has phases like the crescent that marks the start of a Hijri month.
explorations.prompt: Write your idea: As the ball 'Moon' moves around your head, will the lit part you see stay the same, or change? When will you see a full bright circle?
explorations.answer: Did the amount of light on the ball actually change, or did the amount you could SEE change? Explain. At which position did you see a thin crescent? Why is this phase special for the Islamic calendar? Why does the Moon never truly change its size or shape, even though it looks different each night?…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To model how the Moon appears to change shape, and to see why the Moon has phases like the crescent that marks the start of a Hijri month.

explorations.title: The Travelling Shadow (A Home Sundial)
explorations.context: To show that Earth's rotation makes shadows move and change length through the day, without ever looking at the Sun.
explorations.prompt: Write your idea: Will the shadow of a stick stay in the same place all day, or move? When do you think the shadow will be shortest?
explorations.answer: Did the shadow move during the day? What does this tell you about the Sun's position? When was the shadow shortest? Where was the Sun in the sky at that time? The shadow moves because the Sun appears to move. What is REALLY moving to cause this? How is your stick and its moving shadow like an…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To show that Earth's rotation makes shadows move and change length through the day, without ever looking at the Sun.

explorations.title: Keeping a Moon Diary
explorations.context: To observe the real Moon over several weeks and discover the pattern of its phases with your own eyes.
explorations.prompt: Write your idea: Over two weeks, how do you think the Moon's shape will change - grow, shrink, or stay the same?
explorations.answer: Look back over your drawings. Did the lit part grow, shrink, or both over your two weeks? Can you find the crescent, quarter and full phases in your diary? Using the pattern you found, predict what the Moon will look like one week after your last drawing. About how many days did it take to go from…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To observe the real Moon over several weeks and discover the pattern of its phases with your own eyes.

methods.title: Making Day and Night With a Ball and a Torch
methods.example: To model how Earth's spinning causes day and night, and why the Sun seems to move across the sky.
steps: Make the room as dark as you can. Ask an adult to help.
Put a sticker (or draw a small dot) on the ball. This spot is your town, for example Mogadishu.
Hold the torch still and shine it on one side of the ball from about an arm's length away. This is the Sun's light.
Notice that only the side facing the torch is lit. That side has day; the shadowed side has night.
Slowly turn the ball from left to right (west to east), keeping the torch still.

methods.title: Modelling the Phases of the Moon
methods.example: To model how the Moon appears to change shape, and to see why the Moon has phases like the crescent that marks the start of a Hijri month.
steps: Darken the room. Place the lamp on a table, or ask someone to hold the torch, shining steadily from one side of the room.
Stand in the middle. Your head is Earth. Hold the pale ball out at arm's length - this is the Moon.
Turn to face the lamp and hold the ball up towards it. The lit side of the ball faces away from you - this is the NEW MOON (you see almost no light).
Slowly turn on the spot, carrying the ball around your head. Watch a thin lit curve appear on one edge - this is the WAXING CRESCENT.
Keep turning. When you have turned a quarter way round, you see half the ball lit - FIRST QUARTER.

methods.title: The Travelling Shadow (A Home Sundial)
methods.example: To show that Earth's rotation makes shadows move and change length through the day, without ever looking at the Sun.
steps: Early in the morning, stand the stick upright in a sunny spot where it will stay all day. Do NOT move it.
Look only at the SHADOW - never at the Sun. Place a small stone at the very tip of the shadow and note the time.
Every hour or two, come back and mark the new position of the shadow tip with another stone, writing the time beside it.
Continue through the day - morning, midday, and afternoon.
At the end of the day, look at the row of marks and the lengths of the shadows.

methods.title: Keeping a Moon Diary
methods.example: To observe the real Moon over several weeks and discover the pattern of its phases with your own eyes.
steps: Each clear evening, go outside with an adult after sunset and find the Moon.
Draw the exact shape of the lit part of the Moon that you can see.
Write the date beside your drawing, and note roughly what time it was.
Repeat every clear night for at least two weeks - longer is even better.
If a night is cloudy, simply write 'cloudy' and try again the next night.

workedExamples.title: Thinking With Numbers
workedExamples.prompt: Earth and space are full of patterns you can work out with simple arithmetic. Let us do some together, step by step.
workedExamples.solution: Worked Example 1 - How many degrees does Earth turn each hour? Earth makes one full turn - that is 360 degrees - in 24 hours. To find how much it turns in one hour, we divide: 360 degrees divided by 24 hours = 15 degrees per hour. So every hour, Earth spins 15 degrees, and the Sun appears to move 15 degrees across the sky. This is the reason each time zone is about 15 degrees of longitude wide.

workedExamples.title: 1 - How many degrees does Earth turn each hour?
workedExamples.prompt: Earth makes one full turn - that is 360 degrees - in 24 hours. To find how much it turns in one hour, we divide:
workedExamples.solution: 360 degrees divided by 24 hours = 15 degrees per hour. So every hour, Earth spins 15 degrees, and the Sun appears to move 15 degrees across the sky. This is the reason each time zone is about 15 degrees of longitude wide. Worked Example 2 - How long until the next full moon? The Moon's full cycle of phases takes about 29.5 days. Suppose tonight is a new moon. A full moon happens halfway through the cycle. So we work out half of 29.5:

workedExamples.title: 2 - How long until the next full moon?
workedExamples.prompt: The Moon's full cycle of phases takes about 29.5 days. Suppose tonight is a new moon. A full moon happens halfway through the cycle. So we work out half of 29.5:
workedExamples.solution: 29.5 days divided by 2 = about 14.75 days, which is roughly 15 days. So the full moon will come about 15 days after the new moon. If you keep a moon diary, you can check this yourself! Worked Example 3 - How many times does Earth spin in one year? Earth spins once each day, and there are about 365 days in a year. So in one full journey around the Sun, Earth spins about 365 times. Written out: 1 spin per day multiplied by 365 days = 365 spins per year. That means since your last birthday, the ground beneath you has…

workedExamples.title: 3 - How many times does Earth spin in one year?
workedExamples.prompt: Earth spins once each day, and there are about 365 days in a year. So in one full journey around the Sun, Earth spins about 365 times. Written out: 1 spin per day multiplied by 365 days = 365 spins per year. That means since your last birthday, the ground…
workedExamples.solution: Reading Comprehension - Waris Watches for the Crescent Read this short passage carefully, then answer the questions below. The evening after sunset, Waris climbed with her grandfather onto the flat roof of their house near Berbera. The sky over the Indian Ocean was turning from orange to deep blue. "Tonight," her grandfather said softly, "we look for the new crescent. If we see that thin curve of light, tomorrow begins a new month." Waris searched the western sky where the Sun had just set. For a while she saw…

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: Which planet do we live on? (a) the first from the Sun (b) the second (c) the third (d) the fourth
workedExamples.solution: 5 (c) the third. Earth is the third planet out from the Sun.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Why does the Moon shine? (a) it makes its own light (b) it reflects the Sun's light (c) it is on fire (d) it reflects Earth's light
workedExamples.solution: 6 (b) it reflects the Sun's light. The Moon makes no light of its own.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: Which phase marks the start of a new month in the Islamic (Hijri) calendar? (a) full moon (b) third quarter (c) new crescent (d) waning gibbous
workedExamples.solution: 7 (c) new crescent. The thin crescent after sunset marks the start of a Hijri month.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: The Sun appears to rise in the ____ and set in the ____. (a) west, east (b) north, south (c) east, west (d) south, north
workedExamples.solution: 8 (c) east, west. Because Earth spins west to east, the Sun appears to rise east and set west.

practice.prompt: What is at the centre of our Solar System? (a) Earth (b) the Moon (c) the Sun (d) a galaxy
practice.answer: 1 (c) the Sun. The Sun sits at the centre and everything else orbits it.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: How long does Earth take to spin around once? (a) 1 hour (b) 24 hours (c) 1 month (d) 1 year
practice.answer: 2 (b) 24 hours. One full spin (rotation) takes 24 hours, which is why a day is 24 hours.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: What causes day and night? (a) the Sun moving around Earth (b) Earth spinning on its axis (c) clouds covering the Sun (d) the Moon blocking the Sun
practice.answer: 3 (b) Earth spinning on its axis. As Earth turns, your side faces the Sun (day) then turns away (night).
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: How long does Earth take to travel once around the Sun? (a) one day (b) one week (c) one month (d) about one year
practice.answer: 4 (d) about one year. One orbit of the Sun takes about 365 days.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which planet do we live on? (a) the first from the Sun (b) the second (c) the third (d) the fourth
practice.answer: 5 (c) the third. Earth is the third planet out from the Sun.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Why does the Moon shine? (a) it makes its own light (b) it reflects the Sun's light (c) it is on fire (d) it reflects Earth's light
practice.answer: 6 (b) it reflects the Sun's light. The Moon makes no light of its own.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which phase marks the start of a new month in the Islamic (Hijri) calendar? (a) full moon (b) third quarter (c) new crescent (d) waning gibbous
practice.answer: 7 (c) new crescent. The thin crescent after sunset marks the start of a Hijri month.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The Sun appears to rise in the ____ and set in the ____. (a) west, east (b) north, south (c) east, west (d) south, north
practice.answer: 8 (c) east, west. Because Earth spins west to east, the Sun appears to rise east and set west.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: What is a star? (a) a large ball of rock (b) a huge ball of hot glowing gas that makes its own light (c) a planet with no moon (d) a piece of the Moon
practice.answer: 9 (b) a huge ball of hot glowing gas that makes its own light. The Sun is our nearest star.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: About how many days does the Moon take to complete all its phases? (a) 7 (b) 15 (c) 29.5 (d) 365
practice.answer: 10 (c) 29.5. The full cycle of phases takes about 29.5 days.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is NOT a planet in our Solar System? (a) Mars (b) Jupiter (c) the Sun (d) Neptune
practice.answer: 11 (c) the Sun. The Sun is a star, not a planet.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Why are stars useful to travellers at night? (a) they give heat (b) they help find direction (c) they cause the tides (d) they make the Moon shine
practice.answer: 12 (b) they help find direction. Certain stars point the way, like a natural compass.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Making Day and Night With a Ball and a Torch
activities.materials: A ball (an orange, a round fruit, or any ball) to be Earth; A torch, or a phone light, to be the Sun; A small sticker or a dot of marker pen to mark your town; A darkened room
steps: Make the room as dark as you can. Ask an adult to help.
Put a sticker (or draw a small dot) on the ball. This spot is your town, for example Mogadishu.
Hold the torch still and shine it on one side of the ball from about an arm's length away. This is the Sun's light.
Notice that only the side facing the torch is lit. That side has day; the shadowed side has night.
Slowly turn the ball from left to right (west to east), keeping the torch still.

activities.title: Modelling the Phases of the Moon
activities.materials: A small pale ball (a white or light-coloured ball, or a ball wrapped in white paper) to be the Moon; A bright torch or lamp to be the Sun; A darkened room; Your own head, which will stand for Earth
steps: Darken the room. Place the lamp on a table, or ask someone to hold the torch, shining steadily from one side of the room.
Stand in the middle. Your head is Earth. Hold the pale ball out at arm's length - this is the Moon.
Turn to face the lamp and hold the ball up towards it. The lit side of the ball faces away from you - this is the NEW MOON (you see almost no light).
Slowly turn on the spot, carrying the ball around your head. Watch a thin lit curve appear on one edge - this is the WAXING CRESCENT.
Keep turning. When you have turned a quarter way round, you see half the ball lit - FIRST QUARTER.

activities.title: The Travelling Shadow (A Home Sundial)
activities.materials: A straight stick, pencil or stone that stands upright; A sunny patch of ground or a piece of paper on a sunny windowsill; Small stones or a pencil to mark the shadow tip; A watch or clock
steps: Early in the morning, stand the stick upright in a sunny spot where it will stay all day. Do NOT move it.
Look only at the SHADOW - never at the Sun. Place a small stone at the very tip of the shadow and note the time.
Every hour or two, come back and mark the new position of the shadow tip with another stone, writing the time beside it.
Continue through the day - morning, midday, and afternoon.
At the end of the day, look at the row of marks and the lengths of the shadows.

activities.title: Keeping a Moon Diary
activities.materials: A notebook or sheet of paper; A pencil; A clear view of the evening sky, with an adult; A calendar
steps: Each clear evening, go outside with an adult after sunset and find the Moon.
Draw the exact shape of the lit part of the Moon that you can see.
Write the date beside your drawing, and note roughly what time it was.
Repeat every clear night for at least two weeks - longer is even better.
If a night is cloudy, simply write 'cloudy' and try again the next night.

activities.title: Observe: Stars, Constellations and Finding Your Way
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of stars, constellations and finding your way around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Sort: Exploring Space
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of exploring space around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

terms: Earth's orbit (one trip round the Sun)
Earth's natural satellite. It reflects sunlight and takes about 29.5 days to orbit Earth.
The shape of the Moon's sunlit part that we can see from Earth on a given night.
A thin curved sliver of the Moon; the new crescent marks the start of a Hijri month.
Waxing means the lit part is growing; waning means it is shrinking.
A named pattern of stars used to find direction and mark the seasons.
An object that orbits a planet. Natural (the Moon) or built by people (weather satellites).
One of 24 regions of Earth, each about one hour apart, created by Earth's rotation.

vocabulary.example: Earth's rotation takes 24 hours and gives us day and night.
Earth's orbit around the Sun takes one year.

vocabulary.meaning: Earth's natural satellite. It reflects sunlight and takes about 29.5 days to orbit Earth.
vocabulary.example: Year 5 - Unit 6 - Lesson | The Sun, Earth, Moon and the Sky Above You

vocabulary.meaning: The shape of the Moon's sunlit part that we can see from Earth on a given night.
vocabulary.example: A thin crescent phase marks the start of a new Hijri month.

vocabulary.meaning: A thin curved sliver of the Moon; the new crescent marks the start of a Hijri month.
vocabulary.example: When your family watches for the new crescent moon to know that Ramadan or Eid has begun, you are doing exactly what scientists and travellers have done for thousands of years - reading the sky.

vocabulary.meaning: Waxing means the lit part is growing; waning means it is shrinking.
vocabulary.example: A day or two later, a thin bright curve appears - the waxing crescent.

vocabulary.meaning: A named pattern of stars used to find direction and mark the seasons.
vocabulary.example: The stars in a constellation are not really close together - some are far behind others - but from where we stand on Earth they make a shape we can recognise.

vocabulary.meaning: An object that orbits a planet. Natural (the Moon) or built by people (weather satellites).
vocabulary.example: The Moon is a natural satellite, which means it travels around Earth, just as Earth travels around the Sun.

vocabulary.meaning: One of 24 regions of Earth, each about one hour apart, created by Earth's rotation.
vocabulary.example: This is the reason each time zone is about 15 degrees of longitude wide.

commonMistakes: The Sun moves around the Earth.
It looks that way, but only appears to move.
The Moon makes its own light.
The Moon is rock, not glowing gas.
Moon phases come from Earth's shadow.
Earth's shadow only causes rare eclipses.
Seasons come from distance to the Sun.
Earth stays almost the same distance all year.
Stars are smaller than the Sun.
They look tiny because they are far away.

connections.text: the Sun is a light source, and moving shadows are the same idea as a sundial.
Forces (next unit): gravity is the pull that keeps Earth orbiting the Sun and the Moon orbiting Earth.
time zones, seasons, dry and wet seasons, and the equator all link to Earth's motion.
the lunar Hijri calendar, sighting the crescent, and finding the qibla.
working with degrees, days, and simple division to describe the sky.

fluency.errorFeedback: 5 (c) the third. Earth is the third planet out from the Sun.

fluency.prompt: Why does the Moon shine? (a) it makes its own light (b) it reflects the Sun's light (c) it is on fire (d) it reflects Earth's light
fluency.answer: 6 (b) it reflects the Sun's light. The Moon makes no light of its own.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 6 (b) it reflects the Sun's light. The Moon makes no light of its own.

fluency.prompt: Which phase marks the start of a new month in the Islamic (Hijri) calendar? (a) full moon (b) third quarter (c) new crescent (d) waning gibbous
fluency.answer: 7 (c) new crescent. The thin crescent after sunset marks the start of a Hijri month.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 7 (c) new crescent. The thin crescent after sunset marks the start of a Hijri month.

fluency.prompt: The Sun appears to rise in the ____ and set in the ____. (a) west, east (b) north, south (c) east, west (d) south, north
fluency.answer: 8 (c) east, west. Because Earth spins west to east, the Sun appears to rise east and set west.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 8 (c) east, west. Because Earth spins west to east, the Sun appears to rise east and set west.

fluency.prompt: What is a star? (a) a large ball of rock (b) a huge ball of hot glowing gas that makes its own light (c) a planet with no moon (d) a piece of the Moon
fluency.answer: 9 (b) a huge ball of hot glowing gas that makes its own light. The Sun is our nearest star.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 9 (b) a huge ball of hot glowing gas that makes its own light. The Sun is our nearest star.

fluency.prompt: About how many days does the Moon take to complete all its phases? (a) 7 (b) 15 (c) 29.5 (d) 365
fluency.answer: 10 (c) 29.5. The full cycle of phases takes about 29.5 days.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 10 (c) 29.5. The full cycle of phases takes about 29.5 days.

fluency.prompt: Which of these is NOT a planet in our Solar System? (a) Mars (b) Jupiter (c) the Sun (d) Neptune
fluency.answer: 11 (c) the Sun. The Sun is a star, not a planet.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 11 (c) the Sun. The Sun is a star, not a planet.

fluency.prompt: Why are stars useful to travellers at night? (a) they give heat (b) they help find direction (c) they cause the tides (d) they make the Moon shine
fluency.answer: 12 (b) they help find direction. Certain stars point the way, like a natural compass.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 12 (b) they help find direction. Certain stars point the way, like a natural compass.

realProblems.prompt: Halima's family is watching for the crescent moon to know when Ramadan starts. Explain what they are looking for in the sky and why it appears just after sunset in the west.
realProblems.answer: 1. They are looking for the thin new crescent low in the western sky just after sunset. It appears there because the Moon is near the Sun in the sky and only a sliver of its sunlit side faces us, seen briefly before it sets.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1. They are looking for the thin new crescent low in the western sky just after sunset. It appears there because the Moon is near the Sun in the sky and only a sliver of its sunlit side faces us, seen briefly before it sets.

realProblems.prompt: A fisherman near Mombasa notices the sea rises and falls each day. Which object in space helps cause these tides?
realProblems.answer: 2. The Moon. Its gravity pulls on the oceans and is a main cause of the tides.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 2. The Moon. Its gravity pulls on the oceans and is a main cause of the tides.

realProblems.prompt: Omar wants to face the qibla to pray but has no compass. Explain how he could use the setting Sun to work out direction.
realProblems.answer: 3. The Sun sets in the west, so Omar can face the sunset to find west, then work out the other directions and turn towards the qibla.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 3. The Sun sets in the west, so Omar can face the sunset to find west, then work out the other directions and turn towards the qibla.

realProblems.prompt: Amina calls her cousin in a country far to the east and wakes her in the middle of the night. Use Earth's rotation to explain why it was night there when it was afternoon for Amina.
realProblems.answer: 4. When it was afternoon for Amina, her cousin's side of Earth had turned away from the Sun into night. Because Earth is round and spinning, the two places have day and night at different times.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 4. When it was afternoon for Amina, her cousin's side of Earth had turned away from the Sun into night. Because Earth is round and spinning, the two places have day and night at different times.

realProblems.prompt: A farmer near the Jubba River dries grain in the strong midday Sun. Explain why the midday Sun is especially strong near the equator.
realProblems.answer: 5. Near the equator the midday Sun is almost directly overhead, so its light strikes the ground straight on and is very strong, which dries grain quickly.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 5. Near the equator the midday Sun is almost directly overhead, so its light strikes the ground straight on and is very strong, which dries grain quickly.

realProblems.prompt: Earth turns 360 degrees in 24 hours. How many degrees does it turn in one hour? Show your working.
realProblems.answer: 1. 360 degrees divided by 24 hours = 15 degrees per hour.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1. 360 degrees divided by 24 hours = 15 degrees per hour.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain in your own words why the Sun appears to move across the sky, even though it is not really moving around us.
keyIdeas: Earth's orbit (one trip round the Sun)
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1. The Sun appears to move because Earth is spinning. As we ride on the turning Earth, the Sun seems to slide across the sky, just as trees seem to slide past a moving car.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the eight planets in order, starting from the Sun.
keyIdeas: Earth's orbit (one trip round the Sun)
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 2. Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why does the Moon appear to change shape through the month?
keyIdeas: Earth's orbit (one trip round the Sun)
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 3. As the Moon orbits Earth, we see different amounts of its sunlit half, so it appears to change shape through its phases.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between rotation and orbiting? Give an example of each.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 4. Rotation is spinning around an axis (Earth rotating once a day). Orbiting is travelling around another object (Earth orbiting the Sun once a year).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why do people watch for the new crescent moon rather than a full moon to know a new Hijri month has begun?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 5. The new crescent is the first thin sliver of Moon seen just after sunset, which marks the true beginning of a new lunar month; the full moon comes in the middle of the month, not the start.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain why the Moon does not make its own light.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 6. The Moon has no light of its own; it is a ball of rock that reflects sunlight, like a stone lit by a lamp.

questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the Sun. The Sun sits at the centre and everything else orbits it.

questions.question: How long does Earth take to spin around once?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit you will be able to:
I can explain that Earth is a planet that travels around the Sun, together with seven other planets in our Solar System.
I can describe how Earth spinning on its axis causes the cycle of day and night.
I can explain why the Sun appears to move across the sky from east to west, even though the Sun is not really moving around us.
I can describe how Earth's journey around the Sun takes one year (about 365 days).
I can explain why the Moon appears to change shape through its phases, and connect this to the lunar (Hijri) calendar.
I can name the eight planets in order and describe how the Solar System is arranged.
I can use the Sun and stars to work out direction, and understand how people have navigated with the sky.
